payload.description:
## Problem

Most of the site's long-form content is hardcoded as React JSX in `app/(site)/<slug>/page.tsx` files. The CLAUDE.md claims "all long-form content is in MDX" but that is aspirational — only 8 of the ~39 app/(site) routes are MDX-driven (about, bibliography, initial-thesis, living-thesis, master-list, tldr, vocab/bachelor-machines, vocab/pataphysics). The remaining content pages are bespoke TSX, which:

- Blocks non-engineer authoring — every copy edit requires React.
- Blocks the paused LLM-legibility epic (pb-e2f) from exposing real content via /api/agent.
- Drops SEO metadata on half the existing pages (no `generateMetadata` export).
- Forces components/site/site-navigation.ts to be hand-maintained in lockstep with every route change.
- Produces divergent patterns across pages (variance in hero shape, prose wrapping, metadata handling, footer-nav chrome).

## Goal

Cut over to an MDX-native content architecture so that:

1. Every content page is driven by a single MDX file under `content/pages/`.
2. A single catch-all Next.js route `app/(site)/[...slug]/page.tsx` renders any content URL from the matching MDX file.
3. The sidebar is generated procedurally from the `content/pages/` tree. Filesystem hierarchy mirrors URL hierarchy mirrors sidebar structure.
4. Pages are restructured into their conceptual parents (e.g., `/rhonda-shearer-archive` -> `/duchamp/rhonda-shearer/archive`). Old URLs 301 to new URLs.
5. Per-page review via a staging route (`/preview/<slug>` reading from `content/pages-staging/`) lets each migrated page be eyeballed side-by-side against the original before cutover.
6. Page-level table of contents auto-generated from h2/h3 headings in the MDX. Replaces the sidebar anchor-group hack on /the-boxes and /xenotext-theory.
7. Primary nav meta badges (Bitcoin / Ethereum / date / correspondence count) computed from frontmatter.
8. Footer "continue reading" navigation blocks dropped.

## Conventions locked in

- `content/pages/<path>/<slug>.mdx` -> URL `/<path>/<slug>`
- Folder label in sidebar = folder name uppercased with hyphens->spaces (`scratch-writings` -> `SCRATCH WRITINGS`)
- Page label in sidebar = MDX frontmatter `title`
- Parent-link + sibling-folder pattern: `content/pages/foo.mdx` + `content/pages/foo/` -> section with foo link + children from foo/
- Ordering via `content/pages/layout.yaml` at root + optional `layout.yaml` inside each folder
- External links declared in layout.yaml, not in MDX
- Page-level TOC auto-generated from headings; no anchor-groups in sidebar
- Sealed-document verification via `<HashVerification ... />` invoked inline in the MDX body (MDX component registered in the renderer). Hash values live in MDX body props, not frontmatter schema.

## Stays bespoke (NOT migrated)

- `/` (home landing)
- `/gatherer`, `/search`, `/critiques` index, `/vocab` index (tool / listing pages)
- `/bachelor-machines/terms` (already MDX-backed via VocabDoc with a bespoke search-enabled hero; acceptable variance)
- `/xenotext`, `/pataphysics/pataphysics-engine` — to be classified in Phase 0.5

## Execution order

- Phase 0: Infrastructure (5 pebbles). ContentPage doc type, MarkdownRenderer upgrade, TOC component, catch-all route, staging preview route. Coordinator work, sequential within the phase.
- Phase 0.5: Classification (1 pebble). Open /xenotext and /pataphysics/pataphysics-engine, decide per-page whether to migrate or keep bespoke.
- Phase 1: Per-page MDX transcription (22 pebbles). Workers in batches of 4. Each worker transcribes one page into `content/pages-staging/<slug>.mdx`. User reviews `/<slug>` vs `/preview/<slug>`. Coordinator cuts over on approval (moves MDX to final location, deletes original page.tsx).
- Phase 2: Legacy MDX retrofit (6 pebbles). Retrofit the 8 legacy MDX pages (about, bibliography, initial-thesis, living-thesis, master-list, tldr) onto the ContentPage type. InitialThesisDoc / TldrDoc carry hash verification and manuscript display inline in the MDX body.
- Phase 3: Final cutover (3 pebbles). Restructure `content/pages/` into the final hierarchy, add layout.yaml, add redirects, switch sidebar to filesystem-driven, procedurally regenerate sitemap, delete staging route + legacy contentlayer doc types + site-navigation.ts.

## Scope / non-goals

- No visual redesign. Hero + body + TOC is the one layout across content pages; per-page visual tweaks are out of scope.
- Footer "continue reading" blocks on initial-thesis and tldr are deleted, not redesigned.
- VocabDoc pages (`/bachelor-machines/terms`, `/vocab`) retain their bespoke page.tsx wrappers with VocabSearch — out of scope.
- Critique contentlayer type unchanged — critiques are their own category.
- No backwards-compat shims beyond 301 redirects.
- No changes to contentlayer version or Next.js version.

## Relation to paused epic (pb-e2f)

The LLM-legibility epic (feat/llm-legible-content) was paused because its scope assumed all long-form content was in MDX. After this epic lands, pb-e2f can resume — the ContentPage type's required `summary` frontmatter feeds that epic's TOC endpoint directly. The 8 summaries already written on feat/llm-legible-content branches will be brought forward as part of Phase 2 legacy retrofit.

## Production

https://eldenringisthelargeglass.com (Vercel, deploys from dalten-collective/elden-glass: main -> prod, dev -> preview).

Every change lands via PR to dev; production promotion is a separate PR from dev to main.
payload.title: MDX-native content architecture cutover: catch-all route + filesystem sidebar

payload.description:
## Behavioral contract

Add a new `ContentPage` document type to `contentlayer.config.ts` that covers every post-migration content page on the site. Shape:

### Fields

Required:
- `title: string`
- `summary: string` (1-3 sentences, <=280 chars; drives metadata.description AND feeds the paused LLM-legibility epic's TOC)
- `updated: date`

Optional:
- `subtitle: string`
- `eyebrow: string` (the small uppercase tracking-widened label above the title, e.g. "Visual Evidence", "External Source")
- `readingMinutes: number`
- `navMeta: string` (override for the primary-nav meta badge; normally auto-derived from other fields)

Optional sealing fields (presence of `documentHash` is the signal that this is a sealed doc):
- `documentHash: string`
- `hashableFile: string`
- `sealedDate: string`
- `ethereumAttestation: string`
- `bitcoinOts: string`

### Config

- `filePathPattern: 'pages/**/*.mdx'`
- `contentType: 'mdx'`
- `computedFields`:
  - `slug: string` — the file path relative to `pages/`, minus the `.mdx` extension, using forward slashes (e.g. `duchamp/rhonda-shearer/archive` for `content/pages/duchamp/rhonda-shearer/archive.mdx`). This is what the catch-all route will match against.
  - `url: string` — `/` + slug.

Add this doc type to the `documentTypes` array in `makeSource` at the bottom of contentlayer.config.ts.

### lib/content.ts additions

Add the following exports:
- `getContentPageBySlug(slug: string): ContentPage | null` — looks up an MDX doc in `allContentPages` by matching slug. Returns null if not found.
- `allContentPagesSorted(): ContentPage[]` — returns `allContentPages` sorted by slug for deterministic order.
- A helper `withReadingTime` for ContentPage documents (reuse the existing pattern from the file; computes readingMinutes from body.raw if not set in frontmatter).

Preserve all existing exports in lib/content.ts — this pebble is additive only.

## Preconditions

None. This is foundational infrastructure.

## Scope / non-goals

- Do NOT remove or modify any existing contentlayer doc types (InitialThesisDoc, TldrDoc, etc.). Those are retired in Phase 2/3, not now.
- Do NOT add the `headings` computed field here; that is Pebble 0.3's job.
- Do NOT add any MDX files under `content/pages/` yet. This pebble only adds the schema and getters.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `contentlayer.config.ts` exports `ContentPage` and includes it in `documentTypes`.
2. `lib/content.ts` exports `getContentPageBySlug` and `allContentPagesSorted`.
3. `npm run build` passes (contentlayer must accept an empty `pages/` directory without complaint).
4. `npm run typecheck` passes.
5. Adding a test file at `content/pages/_test.mdx` with frontmatter `{ title: 'Test', summary: 'Test summary.', updated: 2026-04-14 }` and a one-line body successfully produces a `ContentPage` doc that `getContentPageBySlug('_test')` returns. Remove the test file before completion.

## Regression gate

Removing `title` or `summary` or `updated` from the test MDX's frontmatter must cause `npm run build` to fail with a contentlayer error naming the offending file. Verify by test, then restore before the commit.
payload.title: Add ContentPage contentlayer doc type + lib/content.ts getters

payload.description:
## Behavioral contract

Update the MDX rendering surface to support the migrated content pages.

### 1. MarkdownRenderer wrapper

In `components/mdx/markdown-renderer.tsx`, change the outer div's className from:

```
'prose prose-invert max-w-none'
```

to:

```
'prose prose-lg prose-invert max-w-none'
```

Rationale: current behavior is that individual page.tsx files sometimes wrap `<MarkdownRenderer>` in their own `prose prose-lg prose-invert max-w-none` section, double-wrapping the prose. Lifting `prose-lg` into the renderer once gives every page consistent typography and lets the legacy pages drop their redundant outer wrappers in Phase 2.

This IS a visual change on pages that currently use bare `<article>` (bibliography, master-list, about). Typography there will render larger. This is accepted.

### 2. Register HashVerification as an MDX component

Import `HashVerification` from `@/components/verification/hash-verification` and add it to the `components` object registered with `useMDXComponent`.

Do not modify hash-verification.tsx itself. It already supports both Bitcoin-only, Ethereum-only, and dual-chain documents.

### 3. Register ManuscriptDisplay as an MDX component

The current location is `@/components/initial-thesis/manuscript-display.tsx` — a server component that reads a file from `public/proofs/` via `node:fs`, masks email addresses, splits header from body.

Move `manuscript-display.tsx` from `components/initial-thesis/` to `components/mdx/` so it lives alongside the other MDX components. Update the existing import in `app/(site)/initial-thesis/page.tsx` to point at the new location (that page will be retired in Phase 2, but must not break in the meantime).

Import it in markdown-renderer.tsx and add it to the `components` object.

### 4. Create EvidencePoint MDX component

Currently `app/(site)/chocolate-grinder/page.tsx` defines an inline functional component `EvidencePoint` (lines 12-34) with signature `{ id: string; title: string; description: string }`. It renders a left-gold-border block with a small tag carrying the id, a title, and a description.

Extract this into `components/mdx/evidence-point.tsx` as a stand-alone component with the same signature. Change the description prop to accept children instead of a `description` string so MDX can pass rich content:

```tsx
export function EvidencePoint({ id, title, children }: { id: string; title: string; children: React.ReactNode }) { ... }
```

Preserve the existing Tailwind classes exactly: `border-l-4 border-[var(--accent-gold)]/50 pl-6 py-2`, `text-xs font-mono text-[var(--accent-gold)] bg-[var(--accent-gold)]/10 px-2 py-1 rounded`, `font-medium text-[var(--text-primary)] mb-1`, `text-sm text-[var(--text-secondary)]`.

Import it in markdown-renderer.tsx and add it to the `components` object.

Do NOT remove the inline EvidencePoint from chocolate-grinder/page.tsx in this pebble — that's the migration pebble's job. The inline version can coexist with the MDX component until migration.

## Preconditions

None. Independent of Pebble 0.1.

## Scope / non-goals

- Do NOT create RelatedLinks or other speculative MDX components. On-demand only.
- Do NOT modify FloatImage, ItemCard, ArtworkCard, LinkPreview, MagnifierImage, DefinitionItem.
- Do NOT add `ManuscriptDisplay`-specific styling changes.

## Acceptance criteria

1. markdown-renderer.tsx's outer div uses `prose prose-lg prose-invert max-w-none`.
2. The `components` map registered with useMDXComponent contains: FloatImage, ItemCard, ArtworkCard, LinkPreview, MagnifierImage, DefinitionItem, HashVerification, ManuscriptDisplay, EvidencePoint.
3. `components/initial-thesis/manuscript-display.tsx` no longer exists; `components/mdx/manuscript-display.tsx` does.
4. `app/(site)/initial-thesis/page.tsx` imports ManuscriptDisplay from `@/components/mdx/manuscript-display`.
5. `components/mdx/evidence-point.tsx` exists and matches the signature + styling above.
6. `npm run build` passes.
7. `npm run typecheck` passes.
8. The 3 existing bare-`<article>` MDX pages (bibliography, master-list, about) render with larger typography on `localhost:3000` — visually verify.
payload.title: Upgrade MarkdownRenderer to prose-lg + register HashVerification, ManuscriptDisplay, EvidencePoint

payload.description:
## Behavioral contract

Add a table-of-contents component that renders the h2 and h3 headings of a content page as a right-rail sticky element on desktop and a collapsible block at the top on mobile. Replaces the sidebar anchor-group hack on `/the-boxes` and `/xenotext-theory`.

### 1. Heading extraction

Add a `headings` computed field to the `ContentPage` contentlayer doc type (defined in Pebble 0.1):

```ts
computedFields: {
  // ... existing slug + url fields
  headings: {
    type: 'json',
    resolve: (doc) => extractHeadings(doc.body.raw),
  },
}
```

Where `extractHeadings(raw: string)` returns an array of `{ level: 2 | 3; text: string; id: string }` by scanning the raw MDX for `^## ` and `^### ` lines. The `id` must match what `rehype-slug` produces for the heading so TOC links anchor correctly. Reuse the `github-slugger` package that `rehype-slug` depends on — check package.json to confirm it's available, install if not. Implementation:

```ts
import GithubSlugger from 'github-slugger';

function extractHeadings(raw: string): Array<{ level: 2 | 3; text: string; id: string }> {
  const slugger = new GithubSlugger();
  const matches = Array.from(raw.matchAll(/^(#{2,3})\s+(.+)$/gm));
  return matches.map((m) => ({
    level: m[1].length as 2 | 3,
    text: m[2].trim(),
    id: slugger.slug(m[2].trim()),
  }));
}
```

Define this function at the top of `contentlayer.config.ts`. A fresh GithubSlugger per resolve call is important — it maintains state to disambiguate duplicates.

### 2. PageToc component

Create `components/site/page-toc.tsx`. Signature:

```tsx
interface PageTocProps {
  headings: Array<{ level: 2 | 3; text: string; id: string }>;
}
export function PageToc({ headings }: PageTocProps) { ... }
```

Render:

- **Desktop (lg+):** a sticky `<aside>` on the right rail of the content area. Width ~220px. Sticky top at a sensible offset (~6rem) below the top bar. Heading list: h2 entries with normal weight, h3 entries indented and smaller. "On this page" header at the top in uppercase tracking-widened accent-gold. If `headings.length === 0`, render nothing.

- **Mobile / narrow (below lg):** a collapsible block at the top of the content. Closed by default. Summary line: "On this page (N)". Expanding reveals the same list. Use a native `<details>` element for simplicity — no JS required.

Each list item is a link to `#${id}`. Clicking smooth-scrolls to the anchor (CSS `scroll-behavior: smooth` on the parent is already sufficient if set at :root; confirm by checking `app/globals.css` and add it there if missing).

Use existing design tokens (`--accent-gold`, `--text-primary`, `--text-secondary`, `--border-subtle`) and match visual weight of the existing sidebar.

Keep the component as a **server component** — it's pure presentation of the passed `headings` array.

### 3. Integration point

The catch-all route (Pebble 0.4) will consume this. Arrange the layout so the TOC lives in a two-column flex/grid on desktop: prose on the left (`max-w-prose` or the existing `max-w-4xl`), TOC on the right. Exact layout implementation is Pebble 0.4's concern; this pebble only delivers the standalone component.

## Preconditions

- Pebble 0.1 complete (ContentPage type exists so the `headings` computed field can be added to it).

## Scope / non-goals

- Do NOT modify any existing page.tsx files (catch-all integration is Pebble 0.4).
- Do NOT add TOC to legacy pages in this pebble (Phase 2 retrofit does that).
- No scrollspy highlighting of the current section (can be a follow-up enhancement).

## Acceptance criteria

1. `components/site/page-toc.tsx` exists and exports `PageToc`.
2. `contentlayer.config.ts` exports the `extractHeadings` helper and `ContentPage.computedFields.headings` produces the right array shape.
3. Rendering `<PageToc headings={[{level: 2, text: "Hello", id: "hello"}]} />` outputs a sticky aside on desktop with one link, and a `<details>` on mobile.
4. Clicking a TOC link navigates to `#<id>` and anchors to the heading with matching ID.
5. `npm run build` passes.
6. `npm run typecheck` passes.

## Regression gate

For an MDX file with headings `## Section A`, `## Section B`, `## Section A` (duplicate), the extracted headings must have IDs `section-a`, `section-b`, `section-a-1` — matching rehype-slug's disambiguation. Verify with a spot-check MDX.
payload.title: Add page-level TOC component auto-extracted from MDX headings
Add catch-all content route app/(site)/[...slug]/page.tsx

payload.description:
## Behavioral contract Add a parallel catch-all route at `/preview/` that reads MDX from a staging directory `content/pages-staging/` instead of `content/pages/`. Workers write new MDX to staging; users review via `/preview/` side-by-side with the original `/` (which still renders from the legacy hardcoded TSX). Coordinator cuts over on approval by moving the MDX from staging to `content/pages/` and deleting the old page.tsx. ### Route location `app/(site)/preview/[...slug]/page.tsx` ### Contentlayer setup Add a second document type `StagingContentPage` to `contentlayer.config.ts`. Identical to `ContentPage` in schema and computed fields, but: - `filePathPattern: 'pages-staging/**/*.mdx'` - Add to `documentTypes` in makeSource This gives you `allStagingContentPages` in contentlayer/generated. Expose via `lib/content.ts`: - `getStagingContentPageBySlug(slug: string): StagingContentPage | null` - `allStagingContentPagesSorted(): StagingContentPage[]` ### Route behavior Identical rendering to the production catch-all (Pebble 0.4), but: - reads from `getStagingContentPageBySlug` - shows a prominent top banner indicating "PREVIEW — staging content" with an accent-gold background strip across the top of the hero, so reviewers never mistake a preview for production - `generateStaticParams` returns staging pages - metadata.robots is `noindex` so search engines never index preview URLs Example banner to include above the hero: ```tsx
Preview — Staging Content
``` ### Directory setup Create `content/pages-staging/.gitkeep` so the directory exists in git even when empty. This is where workers write their transcriptions during Phase 1. ## Preconditions - Pebbles 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4 ## Scope / non-goals - This route is temporary. Pebble 3.3 (final cleanup) deletes it along with the staging directory after all migrations are approved. - Do NOT add any staging MDX content in this pebble. Workers will add it in Phase 1. - Do NOT rewrite the production catch-all to dual-read. Two separate routes, two separate contentlayer types. ## Acceptance criteria 1. `app/(site)/preview/[...slug]/page.tsx` exists. 2. `contentlayer.config.ts` defines `StagingContentPage`. 3. `lib/content.ts` exports `getStagingContentPageBySlug` + `allStagingContentPagesSorted`. 4. `content/pages-staging/.gitkeep` exists. 5. Dropping `content/pages-staging/_test.mdx` with valid frontmatter makes `/preview/_test` render with: - the yellow preview banner above the hero - all hero + body + TOC behavior identical to the production catch-all - `` in the HTML head 6. `npm run build` passes. 7. `npm run typecheck` passes. Remove the `_test.mdx` before the commit.
payload.title: Add staging preview route app/(site)/preview/[...slug]/page.tsx

payload.description:
## Behavioral contract

Open two hardcoded TSX pages whose nature is ambiguous from name alone and decide per-page whether to migrate to MDX or keep as a bespoke tool page.

### Routes to classify

1. `app/(site)/xenotext/page.tsx` — sidebar labels it "Xenotext Cipher (Interactive)". Likely an interactive cipher tool. If client-side state / forms / computed output, classify as TOOL and leave bespoke.
2. `app/(site)/pataphysics/pataphysics-engine/page.tsx` — sidebar labels it "A 'Pataphysics Engine". Could be either. Open to classify.

For each:

- Read the full file.
- If it contains mostly prose interspersed with existing MDX-mappable components (FloatImage, DefinitionItem, evidence callouts, link grids), classify as CONTENT and add a migration pebble for it under this epic with the same contract as the other migration pebbles.
- If it contains React state, event handlers, forms, canvas/SVG interactivity, or tool-specific logic, classify as TOOL and leave out of the migration scope. Add a comment at the top of the page.tsx: `// classified as TOOL by pb-<this-pebble-id>; not migrated to MDX`.

### Output

Report the classification decisions as a comment on this pebble AND either:
- for CONTENT: file a new migration pebble under the epic (pb-695) with the same shape as the existing migration pebbles
- for TOOL: add the classification comment to the top of the respective page.tsx file

## Preconditions

None — independent of Phase 0 infra.

## Scope / non-goals

- Do NOT migrate the content in this pebble even if classified as CONTENT — that happens in the per-page migration pebble.
- Do NOT refactor anything in tool pages.

## Acceptance criteria

1. Both pages read and classified.
2. Classification comment added to any page classified as TOOL.
3. New migration pebble filed for any page classified as CONTENT (with parent=pb-695).
4. `npm run build` still passes (comment addition only is harmless).
payload.title: Classify ambiguous routes (/xenotext, /pataphysics/pataphysics-engine) as content vs. tool

payload.description:
## Behavioral contract

Transcribe the hardcoded page at `app/(site)/astrology/page.tsx` into MDX at `content/pages-staging/astrology.mdx`. The result must render at the preview URL `/preview/astrology` without content loss and visually match the original `/astrology` closely enough for side-by-side review.

### Step 1: Read the whole page

Read the entire `app/(site)/astrology/page.tsx` file. Do not skim. You are transcribing — every paragraph, heading, link, image, and embedded component must land in the MDX or be flagged for review.

Also skim `content/living-thesis.mdx`, `content/initial-thesis.mdx`, and `content/tldr.mdx` once for voice and context if you haven't already. The site is erudite, direct, occasionally wry.

### Step 2: Write the MDX

Create `content/pages-staging/astrology.mdx` with the following frontmatter:

```yaml
---
title: <extracted from the page's <h1> / page-hero-title text, OR from existing metadata.title>
summary: <1-3 sentences, <= 280 chars, describes what an LLM or reader would find here and why. In the site's voice — direct, not marketing>
updated: <today's date, YYYY-MM-DD>
subtitle: <copy from the page-hero-description paragraph if present, else omit>
eyebrow: <copy the small uppercase tracking-widened label above the title if present, e.g. "Visual Evidence", "External Source". Omit if none.>
---
```

Do NOT invent a `readingMinutes` — the ContentPage type computes it from body.raw if omitted.

### Step 3: Transcribe the body

Convert the page's JSX content into MDX prose. Preserve:

- **All <h2>, <h3> headings verbatim** — these drive the auto-generated page-level TOC. Section names are load-bearing.
- **All paragraphs verbatim** — do not paraphrase or condense.
- **All internal links** — use Markdown link syntax `[text](/path)` or inline `<Link href="/path">text</Link>` if the original used Link.
- **All external links** — Markdown syntax, include rel="noreferrer" if relevant.
- **All images** — use `<FloatImage src="..." alt="..." caption="..." />` for captioned images, or standard Markdown `![alt](/path.png)` for plain ones. Confirm external image hosts are in `next.config.mjs` images.domains; if not, flag the host in your completion report (do NOT edit next.config.mjs).
- **All structural callouts**:
  - Evidence-style callouts with id + title + description: use `<EvidencePoint id="..." title="...">description</EvidencePoint>`.
  - Inline definition items: use `<DefinitionItem term="..." definition="..." source="..." />`.
  - Artwork/item cards: `<ArtworkCard ... />` / `<ItemCard ... />` preserving props.
  - Magnifier images: `<MagnifierImage ... />`.
  - Link preview pills: `<LinkPreview ... />`.
  - Blockquotes with attribution: standard Markdown blockquote + em-dash attribution line.
  - Code blocks: triple-backtick fences, preserve language tag.
- **Any HashVerification or ManuscriptDisplay invocation** if present: re-use the MDX component with the same props.

### What to DROP (explicit non-content)

- Footer "Related" / "Continue Reading" / link-grid blocks that reference sibling pages. These are nav concerns handled by the sidebar; they do not belong in content. Drop cleanly.
- Page-frame classNames like `space-y-10`, glass-card wrappers. The catch-all route supplies the hero and prose styling.

### Step 4: Visual verification

Start or keep `npm run dev` running. Visit `/preview/astrology` (not `/astrology` — that still serves the TSX original). Confirm:

- Page renders without runtime errors.
- Hero shows title + any subtitle + eyebrow.
- Body prose matches the original's content structure.
- TOC (right rail on lg+) lists the h2/h3 headings.
- `npm run build` passes.

### What you must NOT do

- Do NOT create or modify `app/(site)/astrology/page.tsx` — coordinator handles cutover on approval.
- Do NOT modify `components/site/site-navigation.ts` — sidebar changes happen at coordinator/end-of-epic.
- Do NOT modify `next.config.mjs`, `app/sitemap.ts`, `contentlayer.config.ts`, or any Phase 0 infrastructure.
- Do NOT modify files outside `content/pages-staging/astrology.mdx`.
- Do NOT commit. Leave the working tree with your staged (or unstaged) MDX. Coordinator commits after approval.
- Do NOT skip content. If you encounter an unmappable React component, STOP and report — do not paraphrase around it.

## Preconditions

- Phase 0 complete (pb-d12, pb-c03, pb-f47, pb-76c, pb-4d5 all closed).
- The preview route `/preview/astrology` is live and reading from `content/pages-staging/`.
- The original `app/(site)/astrology/page.tsx` still exists (required as the transcription source).

## Acceptance criteria

1. `content/pages-staging/astrology.mdx` exists with valid frontmatter (title + summary + updated all filled).
2. `/preview/astrology` renders without errors. Check in browser via `npm run dev`.
3. TOC in the rendered page lists the same h2/h3 headings as the original.
4. All images in the original are present in the MDX (via FloatImage or Markdown).
5. All internal and external links are preserved.
6. `npm run build` passes.
7. `npm run typecheck` passes.
8. Working tree has changes to only `content/pages-staging/astrology.mdx` (plus possibly `content/pages-staging/.gitkeep` already existing). No commits.

## Completion report

Post a comment on this pebble describing:
- The summary you wrote.
- Any mappings you had to make (e.g., "used FloatImage for the Brod-Urban image at /images/...").
- Any content blocks you dropped per the non-content rules above.

```yaml
---

updated: <today's date, YYYY-MM-DD>
subtitle: <copy from the page-hero-description paragraph if present, else omit>
eyebrow: <copy the small uppercase tracking-widened label above the title if present, e.g. "Visual Evidence", "External Source". Omit if none.>
---
```

## Behavioral contract

After all migration pebbles and all legacy retrofit pebbles are complete, every content page lives under `content/pages/` flat. This pebble restructures into the final hierarchical layout that matches the sidebar structure, adds `layout.yaml` files for ordering, and adds 301 redirects in `next.config.mjs` so old URLs forward to new.

### Step 1: Design the final hierarchy

The hierarchy mirrors the current sidebar structure (post-cleanup). Target tree:

```
content/pages/
├── layout.yaml                          # top-level: primary order, section order, loose links, externals
├── living-thesis.mdx                    # primary
├── master-list.mdx                      # primary
├── tldr.mdx                             # primary
├── initial-thesis.mdx                   # primary
├── scratch-writings/
│   ├── layout.yaml                      # optional per-folder order
│   ├── belevan.mdx
│   ├── large-glass-breakdown.mdx
│   ├── duchamp-biography.mdx
│   ├── endings.mdx
│   ├── golden-bough.mdx
│   └── bibliography.mdx
├── pataphysics/
│   ├── layout.yaml
│   ├── pataphysics.mdx                  # parent — pairs with pataphysics/ folder
│   ├── pataphysics-engine.mdx           # only if classified as CONTENT in Pebble 0.6
│   ├── what-is-pataphysics.mdx
│   └── vocab.mdx                        # NOTE: /vocab currently is a bespoke TSX using VocabDoc; if not migrated, skip
├── xenotext-theory/
│   ├── layout.yaml
│   └── xenotext-theory.mdx              # only one MDX; /xenotext stays bespoke unless Pebble 0.6 classifies otherwise
├── cosmology/
│   ├── layout.yaml
│   ├── daisugi-cosmology.mdx
│   └── astrology.mdx
├── bachelor-machines/
│   ├── layout.yaml
│   ├── bachelor-machines.mdx            # parent
│   └── chocolate-grinder.mdx
├── duchamp/
│   ├── layout.yaml
│   ├── rhonda-shearer.mdx               # parent
│   ├── rhonda-shearer/
│   │   ├── impossible-bed.mdx
│   │   └── archive.mdx                  # was rhonda-shearer-archive
│   ├── readymades.mdx                   # parent
│   ├── readymades/
│   │   ├── research.mdx                 # was readymades-research
│   │   └── la-chose-en-soie.mdx
│   ├── chess.mdx                        # parent
│   ├── chess/
│   │   └── research.mdx                 # was chess-research
│   ├── the-boxes.mdx
│   └── duchamp-works.mdx
└── author/
    ├── layout.yaml
    └── about.mdx
```

`/bachelor-machines/terms` stays bespoke (VocabDoc); out of scope here. Same for `/xenotext` and `/pataphysics/pataphysics-engine` unless Pebble 0.6 classified them as CONTENT.

Note: the `_section.yaml` convention proposed earlier for section labels is SUPERSEDED. Per user decision, folder labels are derived mechanically from folder name (kebab-case -> UPPERCASE WITH SPACES). No per-folder label override. The only per-folder YAML is `layout.yaml` for ordering.

### Step 2: git mv the MDX files

One `git mv` per file. Example:

```bash
git mv content/pages/rhonda-shearer-archive.mdx content/pages/duchamp/rhonda-shearer/archive.mdx
git mv content/pages/readymades-research.mdx content/pages/duchamp/readymades/research.mdx
# ... etc
```

Use the full list above as the map from current flat location to target hierarchical location.

### Step 3: Write layout.yaml files

`content/pages/layout.yaml` (root) shape:

```yaml
primary:
  - home       # sentinel — home page is /, no MDX, renders bespoke page.tsx
  - living-thesis
  - master-list
  - tldr
  - initial-thesis
looseLinks:                # top-level links not in any section
  - href: /gatherer
    label: Elden Ring Item Cards
sections:
  - scratch-writings
  - pataphysics
  - xenotext-theory
  - cosmology
  - bachelor-machines
  - duchamp
  - author
externals:                 # external sidebar links declared at nav layer (no MDX source)
  - section: bachelor-machines
    href: https://www.eurogamer.net/an-obituary-for-the-architecture-of-dark-souls-eternally-dying-land
    label: Eternally Dying Land
```

Per-folder `layout.yaml` (where desired) shape:

```yaml
order:
  - bachelor-machines          # parent file first
  - chocolate-grinder
```

If a folder has no layout.yaml, contents sort alphabetically by filename.

### Step 4: Add redirects in next.config.mjs

Add a `redirects` async function to the exported `nextConfig` object. One entry per moved URL:

```js
async redirects() {
  return [
    { source: '/about', destination: '/author/about', permanent: true },
    { source: '/bibliography', destination: '/scratch-writings/bibliography', permanent: true },
    { source: '/belevan', destination: '/scratch-writings/belevan', permanent: true },
    { source: '/large-glass-breakdown', destination: '/scratch-writings/large-glass-breakdown', permanent: true },
    { source: '/duchamp-biography', destination: '/scratch-writings/duchamp-biography', permanent: true },
    { source: '/endings', destination: '/scratch-writings/endings', permanent: true },
    { source: '/golden-bough', destination: '/scratch-writings/golden-bough', permanent: true },
    { source: '/what-is-pataphysics', destination: '/pataphysics/what-is-pataphysics', permanent: true },
    { source: '/daisugi-cosmology', destination: '/cosmology/daisugi-cosmology', permanent: true },
    { source: '/astrology', destination: '/cosmology/astrology', permanent: true },
    { source: '/chocolate-grinder', destination: '/bachelor-machines/chocolate-grinder', permanent: true },
    { source: '/rhonda-shearer', destination: '/duchamp/rhonda-shearer', permanent: true },
    { source: '/rhonda-shearer-archive', destination: '/duchamp/rhonda-shearer/archive', permanent: true },
    { source: '/impossible-bed', destination: '/duchamp/rhonda-shearer/impossible-bed', permanent: true },
    { source: '/readymades', destination: '/duchamp/readymades', permanent: true },
    { source: '/readymades-research', destination: '/duchamp/readymades/research', permanent: true },
    { source: '/la-chose-en-soie', destination: '/duchamp/readymades/la-chose-en-soie', permanent: true },
    { source: '/chess', destination: '/duchamp/chess', permanent: true },
    { source: '/chess-research', destination: '/duchamp/chess/research', permanent: true },
    { source: '/the-boxes', destination: '/duchamp/the-boxes', permanent: true },
    { source: '/duchamp-works', destination: '/duchamp/duchamp-works', permanent: true },
    // Primary 5 stay at their current flat URLs — no redirect needed
    // /gatherer, /search, /critiques, /vocab, /xenotext, /bachelor-machines/terms stay bespoke — no redirect
  ];
},
```

Every redirect is `permanent: true` (emits 308, search-engine safe).

### Step 5: Update all INTERNAL links in the codebase

Any internal href in .tsx/.ts files pointing at an old flat URL (`href="/rhonda-shearer"`, etc.) must be updated to the new hierarchical URL. This includes but is not limited to:
- `components/site/site-navigation.ts` (will be deleted in Pebble 3.2 but still exists when this pebble runs)
- Any page.tsx that links to another page via `<Link href="/...">`
- TopBar, search, etc.

Redirects catch the old URLs for external bookmarks, but internal links should point at the canonical new location.

### Step 6: Verify

- `npm run build` passes.
- `npm run typecheck` passes.
- `npm run dev` — spot check: /duchamp/rhonda-shearer renders; /rhonda-shearer 301s to it; internal navigation from sidebar (still old site-navigation.ts) should update as well.

## Preconditions

- All 22 migration pebbles closed (all content under content/pages/ flat).
- All 6 retrofit pebbles closed (legacy MDX moved to content/pages/ flat).
- Pebble 0.6 classification complete — if /xenotext or /pataphysics/pataphysics-engine classified as CONTENT, those pages are also under content/pages/ flat.

## Scope / non-goals

- Do NOT delete the staging route or content/pages-staging/ (Pebble 3.3).
- Do NOT switch sidebar to filesystem-driven yet (Pebble 3.2). site-navigation.ts still drives the sidebar at end of this pebble, but its href values must be updated to new URLs.
- Do NOT regenerate sitemap yet (Pebble 3.3).

## Acceptance criteria

1. content/pages/ matches the hierarchy above.
2. All layout.yaml files in place.
3. next.config.mjs has `redirects` returning the listed entries. Verify with `curl -I http://localhost:3000/about` returns 308 to /author/about.
4. No internal links still point at old flat URLs. Search the codebase: `grep -rn "href=\"/[a-z]" app/ components/ lib/` should not reveal old paths (except for /gatherer, /search, /critiques, /vocab, /xenotext, /bachelor-machines/terms, and anchor-with-# links).
5. `npm run build` passes.
6. `npm run typecheck` passes.
7. Every migrated page is visitable at its NEW URL and 301-accessible at its OLD URL.

Commit as a single commit with the message summarizing the restructure.

## Behavioral contract

Replace the hand-maintained `components/site/site-navigation.ts` with procedural generation from the `content/pages/` tree.

### Step 1: Implement lib/sidebar.ts

New file `lib/sidebar.ts` exporting a server-side function `buildSidebar()` that returns the same `SiteNavigation` shape currently exported from `components/site/site-navigation.ts` (keep the types; just produce them procedurally).

Algorithm:

1. Read `content/pages/layout.yaml` for root-level configuration:
   - `primary`: ordered list of slugs. For each, look up the ContentPage via `getContentPageBySlug`. Emit a NavLinkItem with href=`/<slug>`, label=doc.title, meta=derived from frontmatter (see Step 2).
   - `looseLinks`: ordered list of `{ href, label }` — emit as NavLinkItem with external or internal href.
   - `sections`: ordered list of folder names. For each, call `buildSection(folder)` recursively.
   - `externals`: list of `{ section, href, label }` — appended to the matching section's children as `NavLinkItem` with `external: true`.

2. `buildSection(folderPath)`:
   - Label = folder name -> uppercase, hyphens -> spaces (`scratch-writings` -> `SCRATCH WRITINGS`).
   - Read `content/pages/<folderPath>/layout.yaml` if present for ordering of items in this folder.
   - Iterate files and subdirectories:
     - For each `<name>.mdx`: look up via contentlayer (slug = the folder path + name), emit NavLinkItem. If a sibling `<name>/` directory exists, wrap: the NavLinkItem becomes the parent, the directory's contents become a NavGroupItem appended after it.
     - For each directory without a sibling `<name>.mdx`: recurse via `buildSection` and emit as a nested NavSectionItem.
   - Sort children by layout.yaml `order` list if present, else alphabetically by filename.

### Step 2: Primary nav meta computation

In `buildSidebar()`, derive `meta` for primary entries from frontmatter:

- `living-thesis`: `meta = formatShortDate(doc.updated)` (reuse existing `formatShortDate` from site-navigation.ts before deletion).
- `master-list`: `meta = ${getMasterListCount()} correspondences`.
- `tldr`: if `doc.bitcoinOts` present -> `meta = 'Bitcoin'`; else null.
- `initial-thesis`: if `doc.ethereumAttestation` present -> `meta = 'Ethereum'`; else null.
- Any primary entry with `navMeta` in frontmatter uses that as an override.

Encode this derivation as part of buildSidebar — no separate data layer.

### Step 3: Wire into SiteShell

Update `components/site/site-shell.tsx`:
- Replace `getSidebarData()` call and the `data` prop plumbing with a direct call to `buildSidebar()`.
- The `Sidebar` component currently takes `data: SidebarData`; change its props to `navigation: SiteNavigation`.
- Remove the `useMemo` + `getSiteNavigation(data)` call in `components/site/sidebar.tsx` — just pass `navigation` through.
- Update `components/site/mobile-sidebar.tsx` (and `top-bar.tsx` if it also consumes SidebarData) to use the new shape.

### Step 4: Delete site-navigation.ts

Once the filesystem-driven builder is wired and working:

```bash
git rm components/site/site-navigation.ts
```

Also delete `getSidebarData()` from `lib/content.ts`. Also delete the `SidebarData` type. Keep `formatShortDate` by moving it into `lib/sidebar.ts`.

### Step 5: Verify

- Start `npm run dev`. Sidebar renders identically to pre-migration — same sections, same order, same labels (with the UPPERCASE section heading change per convention).
- Click through every sidebar link; confirm no 404s.
- `npm run build` passes.
- `npm run typecheck` passes.

## Preconditions

- Pebble 3.1 complete (filesystem hierarchy in place, layout.yaml files exist, redirects set).

## Scope / non-goals

- Do NOT touch critique auto-population logic further — the algorithm above already handles it via contentlayer. The Critiques section is just `content/pages/critiques/` with MDX files auto-emerging as children.
- Wait — currently critiques use the `Critique` contentlayer type (not `ContentPage`). If critiques should remain under their own type, the sidebar builder needs to handle that special case: when encountering `content/pages/critiques/` folder (if we create one), fall through to `allCritiques` from contentlayer. Simpler: critiques stay at `content/critiques/` (their current location, Critique doc type) and the sidebar has a curated "critiques" section defined in the root layout.yaml that pulls from `allCritiques` via a sentinel: `{ section: critiques, dataSource: critiques }`.

  Decision for this pebble: include a special-case in `buildSidebar()` that handles a section with `dataSource: critiques` in the root layout.yaml and pulls from `allCritiques` contentlayer collection. Keep critiques in their existing content/critiques/ path. Unchanged.

- Do NOT delete contentlayer legacy doc types (InitialThesisDoc etc.) — Pebble 3.3.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `lib/sidebar.ts` exists and `buildSidebar()` returns a full SiteNavigation tree.
2. `SiteShell` calls `buildSidebar()`. `Sidebar` component takes `navigation` prop directly.
3. `components/site/site-navigation.ts` does not exist.
4. `getSidebarData()` and `SidebarData` removed from `lib/content.ts`.
5. Sidebar renders all sections/links correctly; folder labels UPPERCASE WITH SPACES.
6. `npm run build` passes.
7. `npm run typecheck` passes.

Commit as a single commit.

## Behavioral contract

Final cleanup: procedurally regenerate `app/sitemap.ts` from contentlayer, delete the staging preview infrastructure, delete the now-dead legacy contentlayer doc types.

### Step 1: Procedural sitemap

Rewrite `app/sitemap.ts` to generate from contentlayer data:

```ts
import type { MetadataRoute } from 'next';
import { allContentPages } from 'contentlayer/generated';
import { getCritiques } from '@/lib/content';

const BASE_URL = process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_BASE_URL || 'https://eldenringisthelargeglass.com';

const staticRoutes = [
  '/',
  '/gatherer',
  '/search',
  '/critiques',
  '/vocab',
  '/xenotext',
  '/bachelor-machines/terms',
];

export default function sitemap(): MetadataRoute.Sitemap {
  const now = new Date();

  const contentPages = allContentPages.map((doc) => ({
    url: `${BASE_URL}${doc.url}`,
    lastModified: doc.updated ? new Date(doc.updated) : now,
    changeFrequency: 'monthly' as const,
    priority: ['', 'living-thesis', 'master-list', 'tldr', 'initial-thesis'].includes(doc.slug) ? 1 : 0.7,
  }));

  const critiquePages = getCritiques().map((c) => ({
    url: `${BASE_URL}/critiques/${c.slug}`,
    lastModified: new Date(c.updated),
    changeFrequency: 'monthly' as const,
    priority: 0.7,
  }));

  const staticEntries = staticRoutes.map((path) => ({
    url: `${BASE_URL}${path}`,
    lastModified: now,
    changeFrequency: 'monthly' as const,
    priority: path === '/' ? 1 : 0.5,
  }));

  return [...staticEntries, ...contentPages, ...critiquePages];
}
```

Verify: visiting `/sitemap.xml` returns an XML doc with every migrated page + all static routes. Compare against the pre-migration sitemap; every URL should still be present (at its new location for migrated pages).

### Step 2: Delete preview route + staging dir

```bash
git rm -rf app/(site)/preview
git rm -rf content/pages-staging
```

### Step 3: Delete StagingContentPage contentlayer type + getters

From `contentlayer.config.ts`: remove `StagingContentPage` definition and from the `documentTypes` array. From `lib/content.ts`: remove `getStagingContentPageBySlug` and `allStagingContentPagesSorted` exports.

### Step 4: Delete legacy contentlayer doc types

From `contentlayer.config.ts`: remove these doc types (they're unused after Phase 2 retrofit):

- `InitialThesisDoc`
- `TldrDoc`
- `LivingThesisDoc`
- `AboutDoc`

This page is a static, prose-heavy article about Seth Giddings's 2007 Games & Culture paper "A 'Pataphysics Engine: Play, Technology, and Realities". No client-side state or interactivity. Sections in order:

1. Hero (eyebrow="Academic Paper", title, subtitle="Play, Technology, and Realities", byline "Seth Giddings, University of the West of England, UK")

6. Key Terms grid (4 items: Ludic Gadgets, The Surrealist Ludic, Liminal/Liminoid, Hyperrealist Ethology)
7. Citation block

payload.title: Rework /duchamp-works into JSON-driven extensible gallery

payload.description:
## Umbrella (this pebble is the tracking parent, not an implementation task)

`/duchamp-works` is a special case inside the `pb-695` MDX cutover epic. Unlike the other 22 Phase 1 pages, it is a structured gallery of Marcel Duchamp's artworks (67 entries, 8 period groups), not long-form prose. The catch-all MDX pattern is the wrong tool here — a flat MDX body cannot efficiently present tabular-ish catalog metadata, per-piece modals, and per-piece optional article links.

This umbrella replaces the original MDX-cutover framing of `pb-695.15`. The rework breaks into three children, each tracked as its own pebble:

1. **Data migration + schema** — move catalog data out of a TypeScript module into validated JSON, enrich the schema with real metadata fields.
2. **Gallery page rewrite** — replace the current "card with title + hover-preview" with a real gallery UI: tile thumbnails, preserved hover-preview, click-to-open modal exposing every present metadata field, optional link to a full MDX article per work.
3. **Author-facing documentation** — a schema reference + editing guide so future additions need no code changes.

## Why this work matters

The site's long-term viability depends on **reducing the marginal cost of new content**. Every time a presentation pattern is hardcoded into a `.tsx` file, the author (a non-engineer collaborator in many cases) is locked out of editing and future changes require engineering effort. By moving catalog data into JSON + Zod schema, and by building a gallery page that renders whatever metadata fields are present per row, we create a **replicable pattern**: any future catalog-style page (other artists, other object sets, scholarship appendices) can reuse the same scaffolding. The author writes data; the page renders it. No new component code per new work.

This is the same principle that drove the pb-695 MDX migration for prose pages — decouple content from presentation, standardize the pipeline, shrink the surface area an author must touch.

## Relationship to `pb-695`

- Parent: `pb-695` (Phase 1 per-page cutover epic)
- This pebble is grafted into Phase 1 because `/duchamp-works` is the Phase 1 pebble that was deferred pending design. It is not a separate epic.
- Closes only when all three children close.

## Non-goals

- This pebble itself writes no code. It is a tracking umbrella.
- Do NOT attempt to fold `/duchamp-works` into the MDX catch-all route. The decision to keep it as a bespoke `page.tsx` is deliberate.

## Behavioral contract

Replace the TypeScript data module at `lib/duchamp-artworks.ts` with a JSON data file validated at read time by a Zod schema. Enrich the per-work schema with real catalog metadata fields so the page can render rich detail when data is present, and degrade gracefully when it is not.

### Required deliverables

1. **New data file**: `data/duchamp-artworks.json`. Must contain every period and every work currently in `lib/duchamp-artworks.ts`, with the existing fields (`title`, `year`, `filename`, period `title`, period `years`) preserved verbatim for all 67 works.

2. **New schema file**: `lib/duchamp-artworks-schema.ts`. Must export a Zod schema and derived TypeScript types. The per-work schema adds the following **optional** fields on top of the existing three:
   - `medium` — string, optional. Example: "Oil on canvas".
   - `dimensions` — string, optional. Example: "54 × 55 cm".
   - `collection` — string, optional. Example: "Philadelphia Museum of Art".
   - `currentLocation` — string, optional. Example: "Gallery 182".
   - `sourceUrl` — string (URL), optional. External canonical page for the work.
   - `note` — string, optional. Short descriptive blurb. **Max 200 words.** Enforce with `.refine` in the schema.
   - `articleSlug` — string, optional. Site-internal slug (e.g. `"impossible-bed-i"`) that resolves via the existing catch-all MDX route. Do NOT include a leading slash. Do NOT validate existence at build time (the author may wire the article later).

3. **New type export**: `types/duchamp-artworks.ts` that re-exports the derived Zod types (`DuchampArtwork`, `DuchampPeriod`). Mirrors the `types/title-cards.ts` convention used by `/gatherer`.

4. **Read-time loader**: a single exported function (location: `lib/duchamp-artworks.ts`, replacing the old module) that imports `data/duchamp-artworks.json`, validates it against the schema, and returns the typed structure. A schema violation must throw at module load — catch it in development, fail the build in production.

5. **Delete the old TypeScript data constants**. `lib/duchamp-artworks.ts` ends this task as a thin loader, not a 240-line data module.

### Acceptance criteria

- `data/duchamp-artworks.json` exists and all 67 works are present with the three legacy fields non-empty where they were non-empty before.
- `npm run typecheck` passes.
- `npm run build` passes.
- `/duchamp-works` still renders the full list after the migration (the page code is untouched in this task — it continues reading the loader; verify by running dev server and loading the page).
- The schema rejects a work whose `note` field exceeds 200 words when parsed — demonstrate with a short unit test (any lightweight test harness or a throwaway script in `/tmp` is acceptable; no new test framework needs to be introduced if none is already present).
- The old const-array export is gone; nothing else in the repo imports the old-style named const directly.

### Scope boundaries / non-goals

- **Do NOT** edit `app/(site)/duchamp-works/page.tsx` in this pebble. That is `pb-695.15b`.
- **Do NOT** populate any of the new optional metadata fields for existing works. The schema adds capacity; backfill is an author task.
- **Do NOT** add an API route. The gallery page can import the loader directly — it is server-component-compatible unless made client by `page.tsx`. Mirroring `/gatherer` pagination is out of scope for 67 rows.
- **Do NOT** move images or change image paths.

### Why

This pebble exists to reduce the marginal cost of maintaining catalog data on the site. Storing structured data in a `.ts` module couples the data layer to the type system and discourages non-engineer authors from editing. Moving to JSON + Zod gives us: (a) author-friendly data editing, (b) runtime validation at the boundary, (c) parity with the existing `/gatherer` pattern (`data/title-cards.json` + `types/title-cards.ts`), and (d) an extensible schema that can grow without touching consumer code. This is the foundation task — every other duchamp-works improvement depends on this schema existing.
